content:
2 min read
Just now

--

Eighteen months ago I made a decision that most people
in the AI space would have called backwards.
Everyone around me was building products. SaaS tools,
GPT wrappers, AI-powered dashboards. The logic was
obvious: build once, sell infinitely, scale without
adding headcount.
I went the other direction. I started taking clients.
Not because I couldn’t build a product. Because I
noticed something that most product builders were
missing entirely.
The Observation
Small businesses weren’t looking for another software
subscription. They were already paying for tools they
didn’t fully use. What they were desperately short of
was someone who could look at their specific operation,
identify the workflow that was quietly costing them the
most, and fix it.
That’s not a product problem. That’s a service problem.
And service businesses, done right, generate something
SaaS companies spend years trying to achieve: predictable
monthly revenue from clients who stay because the thing
you built keeps working.
What I Built Instead
Over the next year I built and maintained automation
systems for small businesses across a handful of
industries — lead qualification bots, content pipelines,
client follow-up sequences, internal reporting workflows.
The tech stack was never exotic. n8n, Make, OpenAI’s API,
Airtable. Visual tools, no custom code. The complexity
was never in the implementation. It was always in the
diagnosis — figuring out which workflow was actually the
load-bearing problem, not just the one the client
described first.
By month eight I had enough retainer clients to cover
what most people would call a full-time income. By month
twelve I had a system — for finding clients, scoping
builds, pricing retainers, delivering automations, and
retaining relationships — that I could describe in
detail from memory.
That’s when I decided to write it down.
Why a Book
Not a course. Not a coaching program. Not a community
with a monthly fee.
A book, because the people I wanted to help didn’t need
forty hours of video content. They needed a clear answer
to one question: how do I actually build an AI automation
agency and make real recurring money from it?
You Don’t Need to Code is 190 pages on exactly that.
It covers the six services small businesses are actively
paying for right now. The discovery call framework that
helps clients find their own number before you quote
anything. The retainer structure that keeps clients for
months, not weeks. The outreach scripts that generate
meetings without a big audience. The delivery process
that makes maintenance predictable instead of chaotic.
And the math. The actual math behind $10K/month — how
many clients, what mix of retainers and project builds,
what average contract value makes the numbers work.
Who This Is For
If you’ve been watching the AI
